questions.prompt: Select all that are true about what an embedding lookup does.
choices: Copies a learned vector row for each token ID
questions.explanation: Each token ID is an address into a learned embedding matrix, so lookup copies one learned vector row per ID. It does not delete order or choose the next word.

questions.prompt: Select all true statements about hidden-state shape at this stage.
choices: The table can feed transformer blocks
questions.explanation: The hidden state is a numeric matrix of token positions by hidden features that can feed transformer blocks. Rows are vectors of numbers, not raw text strings.

questions.prompt: Select all things the embedding table does by itself.
questions.explanation: None are embedding-table responsibilities. Those happen in later model steps.
IDs select rows, positions are added, then rows stack into the matrix.

questions.prompt: Explain the relationship between tokenizer IDs and the embedding matrix.
questions.actual_answer: Tokenizer IDs are stable integer addresses. The embedding matrix is learned model weights with one row per vocabulary ID. Looking up an ID copies that row into the sequence, so the tokenizer and embedding table must agree on the vocabulary order.
questions.rubric: A strong answer names IDs as addresses, the embedding matrix as learned weights, one row per vocabulary ID, and the need for tokenizer/table alignment.
questions.hint: Use the phrase row address if helpful.

questions.prompt: Select all that are true about the hidden-state matrix passed into the first transformer block.
choices: It is a numeric matrix with one row per token position
Its shape is L by D, token positions by hidden features
It is what the transformer block consumes instead of raw text
questions.explanation: All three are true. The matrix has one row per token position, shape L by D, and it is the object the transformer block consumes rather than raw text.
